descriptive_long.question:
5. Answer either (i) or (ii).
(i) Given that the sequence whose \( indexnumb \)th term is \( \left(seriescurr+2 seriesnext\right) \) converges, show that the sequence \( \left\{seriescurr\right\} \) converges also.
(page 306)
(ii) A plane varies so that it includes a cone of constant volume equal to \( \pi fixedscalar^{3} / 3 \) with the surface the equation of which in rectangular coordinates is \( 2 firstcoord secondcoord=thirdcoord^{2} \). Find the equation of the envelope of the various positions of this plane.

State the result so that it applies to a general cone (that is, conic surface) of the second order.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Suppose \( \lim \left(seriescurr+2 seriesnext\right)=3 limitvalue \). Then
\[
\lim \left[\left(seriescurr-limitvalue\right)+2\left(seriesnext-limitvalue\right)\right]=0
\]

Put \( shiftedcurr=seriescurr-limitvalue \); then \( \lim \left(shiftedcurr+2 shiftednext\right)=0 \). We shall prove that \( \lim shiftedcurr=0 \). This will
sequence. Given \( epsilonval>0 \), choose \( startindex \) so that
\[
\left|shiftedcurr+2 shiftednext\right|<epsilonval \quad \text { for all } indexnumb \geq startindex .
\]

By induction on \( stepcount \) we find that
\[
shiftatk-(-2)^{stepcount} shiftkplusp=\sum_{summindex=0}^{stepcount-1}(-2)^{summindex}\left(shiftkplusi+2 shiftkiplusone\right)
\]

Therefore
\[
\left|shiftatk-(-2)^{stepcount} shiftkplusp\right| \leq \sum_{summindex=0}^{stepcount-1} 2^{summindex}\left|shiftkplusi+2 shiftkiplusone\right|<2^{stepcount} epsilonval,
\]
provided \( stepcount \geq 1 \). Dividing by \( 2^{stepcount} \), we get so
\[
\left|shiftkplusp\right|<epsilonval+\frac{1}{2^{j}}\left|shiftatk\right| .
\]

Hence \( \limsup _{stepcount \rightarrow \infty}\left|shiftkplusp\right| \leq epsilonval \).
Therefore
\[
\limsup _{indexnumb \rightarrow \infty}\left|shiftedcurr\right| \leq epsilonval .
\]

Since \( epsilonval \) was arbitrary, this implies \( \lim shiftedcurr=0 \). As we remarked before, this proves that the sequence \( \left\{seriescurr\right\} \) converges to \( limitvalue \).

Remark. The argument generalizes to prove that if \( seriescurr-lambdapar seriesnext \rightarrow areavalue \),
where \( |lambdapar|>1 \), then \( seriescurr \rightarrow areavalue /(1-lambdapar) \).

Solution. Make the change of coordinates
\[
\begin{array}{l}
firstcoord=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{2}(rotcoordu+rotcoordv) \
secondcoord=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{2}(rotcoordu-rotcoordv)
\end{array}
\]

Then the \( rotcoordu rotcoordv \)-axes are orthogonal and rotated by \( \pi / 4 \) radians from the \( firstcoord secondcoord \) axes. The equation of the given surface in the new coordinates is
\[
thirdcoord^{2}+rotcoordv^{2}=rotcoordu^{2}
\]
which is a right circular cone with axis the \( rotcoordu \)-axis.
Next we find the volume of the conical region cut off from the solid cone
by a plane. Because of rotational symmetry we need only consider planes of the form \( rotcoordu=slopeparam rotcoordv+planeoffset \). In order that the plane should cut off a bounded
region it is necessary that \( |slopeparam|<1 \). The region cut off is then a cone with an elliptical base. We will find the altitude of that cone and the area of its base.
The altitude is the distance from the origin to the plane, namely
\[
\frac{|planeoffset|}{\sqrt{1+slopeparam^{2}}}
\]

The area of the base is \( \sqrt{1+slopeparam^{2}} \) times the area of the ellipse obtained by projecting it orthogonally onto the \( \boldsymbol{rotcoordv\, thirdcoord} \)-plane. To find the equation of the
projected ellipse we eliminate \( rotcoordu \) between the equations of the cone and the plane, getting
\[
thirdcoord^{2}+rotcoordv^{2}=(slopeparam rotcoordv+planeoffset)^{2}
\]

Collecting the \( rotcoordv \) terms and completing the square we get
\[
thirdcoord^{2}+\left(1-slopeparam^{2}\right)\left(rotcoordv-\frac{slopeparam planeoffset}{1-slopeparam^{2}}\right)^{2}=planeoffset^{2}\left(\frac{1}{1-slopeparam^{2}}\right)
\]
[Note that this is indeed an ellipse because \( |slopeparam|<1 \) ]. The area of this ellipse is
\[
areavalue=\pi planeoffset^{2} \frac{1}{\left(1-slopeparam^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} .
\]

The volume of the conical region is therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{3} \text { base } \times \text { altitude } & =\frac{1}{3}\left(\sqrt{1+slopeparam^{2}} \pi planeoffset^{2} \frac{1}{\left(1-slopeparam^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}\right)\left(\frac{|planeoffset|}{\sqrt{1+slopeparam^{2}}}\right) \\
& =\frac{1}{3} \pi|planeoffset|^{3} \frac{1}{\left(1-slopeparam^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} .
\end{aligned}
\]

The problem restricts consideration to those planes which cut off a vol-
ume \( \frac{1}{3} \pi fixedscalar^{3} \), that is, to planes for which
\[
|planeoffset|=fixedscalar \sqrt{1-slopeparam^{2}}
\]
where \( slopeparam \) is the tangent of the angle between the plane and the \( thirdcoord rotcoordv \)-plane. We are to find the envelope \( E \) of all such planes. Clearly \( E \) must share
the rotational symmetry of the entire configuration, so it suffices to find the
intersection \( I \) of \( E \) with the \( rotcoordu rotcoordv \)-plane.
Consider the plane \( P \) tangent to \( E \) at a point of \( I \). Reflection in the \( rotcoordu rotcoordv \) plane preserves \( P \), since it preserves \( E \) and fixes each point of \( I \). Therefore \( P \) is perpendicular to the \( rotcoordu rotcoordv \)-plane, so it has an equation of the form \( rotcoordu=slopeparam rotcoordv+planeoffset \) where, as we have seen, \( |planeoffset|=fixedscalar \sqrt{1-slopeparam^{2}} \). The line \( l \) in which \( P \) meets the \( rotcoordu rotcoordv \)-plane is clearly tangent to \( I \). Hence \( I \) is the envelope of all lines \( l \) in the \( rotcoordu rotcoordv \)-plane having equations of the form
(1)
\[
rotcoordu=slopeparam rotcoordv \pm fixedscalar \sqrt{1-slopeparam^{2}} .
\]

The envelope problem is thus reduced to two dimensions.
To find the envelope, differentiate the relation
(2)
\[
rotcoordu=slopeparam rotcoordv \pm fixedscalar \sqrt{1-slopeparam^{2}}
\]
with respect to \( slopeparam \) and eliminate \( slopeparam \) between (2) and
(3)
\[
0=rotcoordv-\frac{fixedscalar slopeparam}{\sqrt{1-slopeparam^{2}}} .
\]

From (3) we find
\[
slopeparam^{2}=\frac{rotcoordv^{2}}{rotcoordv^{2}+fixedscalar^{2}}, \quad \text { so } \quad 1-slopeparam^{2}=\frac{fixedscalar^{2}}{rotcoordv^{2}+fixedscalar^{2}} .
\]

Equation (2) now becomes
\[
rotcoordu=\sqrt{1-slopeparam^{2}}\left(\frac{slopeparam}{\sqrt{1-slopeparam^{2}}} rotcoordv+fixedscalar\right)=\sqrt{1-slopeparam^{2}}\left(\frac{rotcoordv^{2}+fixedscalar^{2}}{fixedscalar}\right),
\]
giving finally
(4)
\[
rotcoordu^{2}=rotcoordv^{2}+fixedscalar^{2}
\]
as the equation of the envelope \( I \). The same equation results if we start with the negative sign.
[Since the algebraic work could introduce extraneous points not in the locus, we should check to see that the given family of lines is exactly the
family of tangent lines to the hyperbola (4). This examination reveals that
the tangents to the upper branch of the hyperbola involve the plus sign in (1), while the tangents to the lower branch involve the minus sign. Thus the lower branch is indeed extraneous to the problem of finding the envelope of the family of lines (2).]
The envelope surface shares the axis of the original cone, namely the \( rotcoordu \) axis; hence its equation is
\[
rotcoordu^{2}=rotcoordv^{2}+thirdcoord^{2}+fixedscalar^{2} .
\]

Transforming back to the original coordinates its equation is
\[
2 firstcoord secondcoord=thirdcoord^{2}+fixedscalar^{2} .
\]

Generalization. Any non-degenerate quadratic cone is affinely equivalent to a right circular cone, and since ratios of volumes are preserved under an affine transformation, the calculations above lead to the conclusion that if all planes of a family cut off solids of the same fixed
volume from a quadratic cone, the planes must be tangent to a hyperboloid which is asymptotic to the cone.

Remark. We have seen that all planes tangent to a hyperboloid of two sheets cut off the same volume from the asymptotic cone. This is analogous to the fact that all lines tangent to a hyperbola form with the asymptotes. Consider \( H \) the "hyperboloid of two sheets" in \( \mathbf{R}^{indexnumb} \) given by
\[
firstcoord_{1}{ }^{2}+firstcoord_{2}{ }^{2}+\cdots+firstcoord_{indexnumb-1}{ }^{2}=firstcoord_{indexnumb}{ }^{2}-fixedscalar^{2} .
\]

Its asymptotic cone \( C \) has equation
\[
firstcoord_{1}{ }^{2}+firstcoord_{2}{ }^{2}+\cdots+firstcoord_{indexnumb-1}{ }^{2}=firstcoord_{indexnumb}{ }^{2} .
\]

If \( P \) is any hyperplane tangent to \( H \), then \( P \) and \( C \) divide \( \mathbf{R}^{indexnumb} \) into five regions (seven, if \( indexnumb=2 \)) of which only one is bounded. The \( indexnumb \)-dimensional
volume of this bounded region \( B_{P} \) is the same for all choices of \( P \). The group of linear transformations preserving the quadratic form \( firstcoord_{1}{ }^{2}+firstcoord_{2}{ }^{2}+\cdots+firstcoord_{indexnumb-1}{ }^{2}-firstcoord_{indexnumb}{ }^{2} \) acts transitively on the set of hyperplanes tangent to \( H \) and is volume-preserving; hence all the regions \( B_{P} \) have the same
volume.

As in the case of three dimensions, all "hyperboloids of two sheets" (i.e.,
non-degenerate, disconnected quadric surfaces) in \( \mathbf{R}^{indexnumb} \) are equivalent to \( H \)
under a linear transformation.

5. Answer either (i) or (ii).
(i) Given that the sequence whose \( batteryage \)th term is \( \left(compassdust+2 gardenloom\right) \) converges, show that the sequence \( \left\{compassdust\right\} \) converges also.
(page 306)
(ii) A plane varies so that it includes a cone of constant volume equal to \( \pi zeppelinjar^{3} / 3 \) with the surface the equation of which in rectangular coordinates is \( 2 tabletfern umbrellaoak=violinrope^{2} \). Find the equation of the envelope of the various positions of this plane.

State the result so that it applies to a general cone (that is, conic surface) of the second order.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Suppose \( \lim \left(compassdust+2 gardenloom\right)=3 cactusmoss \). Then
\[
\lim \left[\left(compassdust-cactusmoss\right)+2\left(gardenloom-cactusmoss\right)\right]=0
\]

Put \( hammockrice=compassdust-cactusmoss \); then \( \lim \left(hammockrice+2 lanternfoil\right)=0 \). We shall prove that \( \lim hammockrice=0 \). This will
sequence. Given \( flamingoice> 0 \), choose \( saddlemug \) so that
\[
\left|hammockrice+2 lanternfoil\right|<flamingoice \quad \text { for all } batteryage \geq saddlemug .
\]

By induction on \( quartzseed \) we find that
\[
pocketdrum-(-2)^{quartzseed} marbletwig=\sum_{ribbonflax=0}^{quartzseed-1}(-2)^{ribbonflax}\left(napkinfate+2 orchardveil\right)
\]

Therefore
\[
\left|pocketdrum-(-2)^{quartzseed} marbletwig\right| \leq \sum_{ribbonflax=0}^{quartzseed-1} 2^{ribbonflax}\left|napkinfate+2 orchardveil\right|<2^{quartzseed} flamingoice,
\]
provided \( quartzseed \geq 1 \). Dividing by \( 2^{quartzseed} \), we get
so
\[
\left|marbletwig\right|<flamingoice+\frac{1}{2^{j}}\left|pocketdrum\right| .
\]

Hence
\( \limsup _{quartzseed \rightarrow-\infty}\left|marbletwig\right| \leq flamingoice \).
Therefore
\[
\limsup _{batteryage \rightarrow \infty}\left|hammockrice\right| \leq flamingoice .
\]

Since \( flamingoice \) was arbitrary, this implies \( \lim hammockrice=0 \). As we remarked before, this proves that the sequence \( \left\{compassdust\right\} \) converges to \( cactusmoss \).

Remark. The argument generalizes to prove that if \( compassdust-dolphinreed gardenloom \rightarrow emeraldsock \), where \( |dolphinreed|>1 \), then \( compassdust \rightarrow emeraldsock /(1-dolphinreed) \).

Solution. Suppose \( \lim \left(divergent+2 divergentnext\right)=3 startpoint \). Then
\[\lim \left[\left(divergent-startpoint\right)+2\left(divergentnext-startpoint\right)\right]=0\]

Put \( constant=divergent-startpoint \); then \( \lim \left(constant+2 constantnext\right)=0 \). We shall prove that \( \lim constant=0 \). This will sequence. Given \( gigantic>0 \), choose \( selector \) so that
\[\left|constant+2 constantnext\right|<gigantic \quad \text { for all } continuous \geq selector .\]

By induction on \( aggregate \) we find that
\[constantbase-(-2)^{aggregate} constantoffset=\sum_{totality=0}^{aggregate-1}(-2)^{totality}\left(constantinner+2 constantinnernext\right)\]

Therefore
\[\left|constantbase-(-2)^{aggregate} constantoffset\right| \leq \sum_{totality=0}^{aggregate-1} 2^{totality}\left|constantinner+2 constantinnernext\right|<2^{aggregate} gigantic,\]
provided \( aggregate \geq 1 \). Dividing by \( 2^{aggregate} \), we get so
\[\left|constantoffset\right|<gigantic+\frac{1}{2^{j}}\left|constantbase\right| .\]

Hence \( \limsup _{aggregate \rightarrow-\infty}\left|constantoffset\right| \leq gigantic \). Therefore
\[\limsup _{continuous \rightarrow \infty}\left|constant\right| \leq gigantic .\]

Since \( gigantic \) was arbitrary, this implies \( \lim constant=0 \). As we remarked before, this proves that the sequence \( \left\{divergent\right\} \) converges to \( startpoint \).

Remark. The argument generalizes to prove that if \( divergent-stability divergentnext \rightarrow emptiness \), where \( |stability|>1 \), then \( divergent \rightarrow emptiness /(1-stability) \).

Solution. Make the change of coordinates
\[\begin{array}{l}

certainty=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{2}(orthogonal-stationary)
\end{array}\]

Then the \( orthogonal stationary \)-axes are orthogonal and rotated by \( \pi / 4 \) radians from the \( knownvalue certainty \) axes. The equation of the given surface in the new coordinates is
\[horizontal^{2}+stationary^{2}=orthogonal^{2}\]
which is a right circular cone with axis the \( orthogonal \)-axis.

Next we find the volume of the conical region cut off from the solid cone by a plane. Because of rotational symmetry we need only consider planes of the form \( orthogonal=flatness stationary+originpoint \). In order that the plane should cut off a bounded region it is necessary that \( |flatness|<1 \). The region cut off is then a cone with an elliptical base. We will find the altitude of that cone and the area of its base.
The altitude is the distance from the origin to the plane, namely
\[\frac{|originpoint|}{\sqrt{1+flatness^{2}}}\]

The area of the base is \( \sqrt{1+flatness^{2}} \) times the area of the ellipse obtained by projecting it orthogonally onto the \( stationary horizontal \)-plane. To find the equation of the projected ellipse we eliminate \( orthogonal \) between the equations of the cone and the plane, getting
\[horizontal^{2}+stationary^{2}=(flatness stationary+originpoint)^{2}\]

Collecting the \( stationary \) terms and completing the square we get
\[horizontal^{2}+\left(1-flatness^{2}\right)\left(stationary-\frac{flatness originpoint}{1-flatness^{2}}\right)^{2}=originpoint^{2}\left(\frac{1}{1-flatness^{2}}\right)\]
[Note that this is indeed an ellipse because \( |flatness|<1 \).] The area of this ellipse is
\[A=\pi originpoint^{2} \frac{1}{\left(1-flatness^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} .\]

The volume of the conical region is therefore
\[\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{3} \text { base } \times \text { altitude } &=\frac{1}{3}\left(\sqrt{1+flatness^{2}} \pi originpoint^{2} \frac{1}{\left(1-flatness^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}\right)\left(\frac{|originpoint|}{\sqrt{1+flatness^{2}}}\right) \\
&=\frac{1}{3} \pi|originpoint|^{3} \frac{1}{\left(1-flatness^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} .
\end{aligned}\]

The problem restricts consideration to those planes which cut off a volume \( \frac{1}{3} \pi variable^{3} \), that is, to planes for which
\[|originpoint|=variable \sqrt{1-flatness^{2}}\]
where \( flatness \) is the tangent of the angle between the plane and the \( horizontal stationary \)-plane. We are to find the envelope \( E \) of all such planes. Clearly \( E \) must share the rotational symmetry of the entire configuration, so it suffices to find the intersection \( I \) of \( E \) with the \( orthogonal stationary \)-plane.

Consider the plane \( P \) tangent to \( E \) at a point of \( I \). Reflection in the \( orthogonal stationary \) plane preserves \( P \), since it preserves \( E \) and fixes each point of \( I \). Therefore \( P \) is perpendicular to the \( orthogonal stationary \)-plane, so it has an equation of the form \( orthogonal=flatness stationary+originpoint \) where, as we have seen, \( |originpoint|=variable \sqrt{1-flatness^{2}} \). The line \( l \) in which \( P \) meets the \( orthogonal stationary \)-plane is clearly tangent to \( I \). Hence \( I \) is the envelope of all lines \( l \) in the \( orthogonal stationary \)-plane having equations of the form
(1)
\[orthogonal=flatness stationary \pm variable \sqrt{1-flatness^{2}} .\]

The envelope problem is thus reduced to two dimensions. Differentiating implicitly, we obtain
(3)
\[0=stationary-\frac{variable flatness}{\sqrt{1-flatness^{2}}} .\]

From (3) we find
\[flatness^{2}=\frac{stationary^{2}}{stationary^{2}+variable^{2}}, \quad \text { so } \quad 1-flatness^{2}=\frac{variable^{2}}{stationary^{2}+variable^{2}} .\]

Substituting, we finally get
(4)
\[orthogonal^{2}=stationary^{2}+variable^{2}\]
as the equation of the envelope \( I \).

Restoring the third dimension, the envelope has equation
\[orthogonal^{2}=stationary^{2}+horizontal^{2}+variable^{2}\]
which in the original coordinates becomes
\[2 knownvalue certainty=horizontal^{2}+variable^{2} .\]

\[knownvalue_{1}{ }^{2}+knownvalue_{2}{ }^{2}+\cdots+knownvalue_{continuous-1}{ }^{2}=knownvalue_{continuous}{ }^{2} .\]

If \( P \) is any hyperplane tangent to \( H \), then \( P \) and \( C \) divide \( \mathbf{R}^{continuous} \) into five regions (seven, if \( continuous=2 \) ) of which only one is bounded. \[
\textbf{5.\; Answer either {\rm(i)} or {\rm(ii)}.}
\]

\medskip
\textbf{(i)\; Functional-analytic variant - uniform formulation}

Let $B$ be a complex Banach space, fix an integer $m\ge 1$, and choose
complex numbers $c_{0},c_{1},\dots ,c_{m}$, not all zero.  Given a
$B$-valued sequence $(s_{n})_{n\ge 0}\subset B$ define
\[
P(z)=\sum_{k=0}^{m} c_{k}z^{k},\qquad 
           R_{n}:=\sum_{k=0}^{m}c_{k}s_{\,n+k}\qquad(n\ge 0).
\]
Assume  

\quad$\bullet$ every zero $\lambda$ of $P$ satisfies $\lvert\lambda\rvert>1$
(so in particular $P(1)\neq 0$);  

\quad$\bullet$ the Banach-valued sequence $(R_{n})$ converges in norm to a
limit $L\in B$.

\smallskip
(a) Prove that $(s_{n})$ converges in $B$ and that
\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}s_{n}\;=\;\frac{L}{P(1)}.
\]

(b) Show that the spectral hypothesis $\lvert\lambda\rvert>1$ is sharp by
constructing an explicit pair
\[
(s_{n})_{n\ge 0}\subset\mathbf C,\qquad P\in\mathbf C[z]
\]
with at least one root on the unit circle for which $(R_{n})$ converges
while $(s_{n})$ does \emph{not} converge.

\bigskip
\textbf{(ii)\; Higher-dimensional geometry - fully corrected formulation}

Throughout $\rho:=\sqrt{x_{1}^{2}+x_{2}^{2}+x_{3}^{2}}$.

\medskip
\textbf{(a)\; The four-dimensional model.}

In $\mathbf R^{4}$ consider the \emph{quadric cone surface}
\[
C\;:\;x_{1}^{2}+x_{2}^{2}+x_{3}^{2}=x_{4}^{2},\qquad x_{4}\ge 0,
\]
and its solid cone
\[
\mathcal K:=\{x\in\mathbf R^{4}\;:\;\rho\le x_{4},\;x_{4}\ge 0\}.  \tag{$\ast$}
\]
For a unit vector $u\in\mathbf R^{4}$ and $b>0$ let
\[
\Pi_{u,b}:\;u\!\cdot\!x=b, \tag{$\dagger$}
\]
and define the truncated solid
\[
\Omega(u,b):=\mathcal K\cap\bigl\{u\!\cdot\!x\le b\bigr\}.
\]
Suppose that \emph{every} hyperplane that meets $\mathcal K$ in a bounded
region cuts off the same four-volume
\[
\operatorname{vol}_{4}\!\bigl(\Omega(u,b)\bigr)=
V=\frac{\pi^{2}a^{4}}{4}\qquad (a>0). \tag{$\ddagger\ddagger$}
\]

(i)   Using the $\operatorname{O}(3)$ symmetry in the first three coordinates,
      show that the normal vector may be chosen as
      \[
         u_{\theta}=(\sin\theta,0,0,\cos\theta),\qquad -\tfrac{\pi}{4}<\theta<\tfrac{\pi}{4}.
      \]
      For the plane $H_{\theta,b}:\sin\theta\,x_{1}+\cos\theta\,x_{4}=b$ prove,
      by an explicit four-volume computation, that $(\ddagger\ddagger)$ is
      equivalent to
      \[
         b^{4}=\frac{3\pi a^{4}}{4}\,
               \frac{(1-\tan^{2}\theta)^{2}}
                    {(1+\tan^{2}\theta)^{2}}. \tag{$\ddagger$}
      \]

(ii)  Put $m:=-\tan\theta$ ($\lvert m\rvert<1$), so that $b=b(m)$ is fixed by
      $(\ddagger)$.  Rotating $H_{\theta,b}$ about the $x_{4}$-axis gives the
      one-parameter family of hyperplanes  
      \[
        \sin\theta\,(n\!\cdot\!x')+\cos\theta\,x_{4}=b
        \quad\bigl(n\in S^{2},\;x'=(x_{1},x_{2},x_{3})\bigr),
      \]
      whose common envelope is the $C^{1}$ ruled hypersurface
      \[
        \Sigma_{m}:\;x_{4}=m\rho+\sqrt{1+m^{2}}\;b(m). \tag{$\heartsuit$}
      \]
      Eliminate $m$ from the equations
      \[
          F(m):=x_{4}-m\rho-\sqrt{1+m^{2}}\;b(m)=0,
          \qquad
          F'(m)=0,
      \]
      and prove that the \emph{collection} of envelopes $\Sigma_{m}$ has the further
      envelope
      \[
        x_{4}^{2}-\bigl(x_{1}^{2}+x_{2}^{2}+x_{3}^{2}\bigr)=
        \Bigl(\tfrac{3\pi}{4}\Bigr)^{\!1/2}a^{2}. \tag{$\clubsuit$}
      \]

\medskip
\textbf{(b)\; The general quadratic cone.}

Let $n\ge 2$ and let $Q$ be a symmetric, non-degenerate quadratic
form on $\mathbf R^{\,n+1}$ of \emph{Lorentzian} signature $(\,n,1)$.  Define the solid cone
\[
\mathcal K_{Q}:=\bigl\{x\in\mathbf R^{\,n+1}\;:\;Q(x)\le 0,\;x_{\,n+1}\ge 0\bigr\}.
\]
For a unit vector $u\in\mathbf R^{\,n+1}$ and $b>0$ put
\[
\Pi_{u,b}:\;u\!\cdot\!x=b,\qquad
\Omega_{Q}(u,b):=\mathcal K_{Q}\cap\{u\!\cdot\!x\le b\}.

bounded regions, all of which have the same $(n+1)$-dimensional volume $V$.

kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout $\lVert\cdot\rVert$ denotes the norm of the underlying real or
complex Banach space, and $\rho:=\sqrt{x_{1}^{2}+x_{2}^{2}+x_{3}^{2}}$.

\bigskip
\textbf{(i) Functional-analytic part}

\smallskip
\emph{(a) Convergence of $(s_{n})$.}
Put $\delta_{n}:=R_{n}-L$ and
$t_{n}:=s_{n}-L/P(1)$.  Then
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{m}c_{k}\,t_{\,n+k}=\delta_{n}\qquad(n\ge 0). \tag{1}
\]
All zeros of $P$ lie outside the closed unit disc, hence
$1/P$ has a power-series expansion
\[
\frac{1}{P(z)}=\sum_{j=0}^{\infty}a_{j}z^{j},\qquad
|a_{j}|\le C\,r^{-j}\quad\text{for some }C>0,\;r>1. \tag{2}
\]
Convolution of (1) with the sequence $(a_{j})_{j\ge 0}$ yields
\[
t_{n}=\sum_{j=0}^{\infty}a_{j}\,\delta_{\,n+j}\qquad(n\ge 0). \tag{3}
\]
Because $\lVert\delta_{n}\rVert\to 0$ and
$\sum_{j\ge 0}r^{-j}<\infty$, a dominated-tail estimate shows
$\lVert t_{n}\rVert\to 0$, hence
\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}s_{n}=\frac{L}{P(1)}.
\]

\smallskip
\emph{(b) Sharpness of the spectral condition.}
Take $P(z)=1-z$ (whose zero lies on the unit circle) and $s_{n}=n$.  Then
\[
R_{n}=s_{n}-s_{n+1}=-1\qquad(n\ge 0),
\]
so $(R_{n})$ converges, whereas $(s_{n})$ clearly diverges.  Thus the
hypothesis $\lvert\lambda\rvert>1$ cannot be weakened.

\bigskip
\textbf{(ii) Geometric part}

\smallskip
\textbf{(a) Exact four-volume of $\Omega(u_{\theta},b)$.}

Because $\mathcal K$ is invariant under the $\operatorname{O}(3)$ action on the
first three coordinates, we may rotate the hyperplane so that its unit
normal is $u_{\theta}=(\sin\theta,0,0,\cos\theta)$ with
$|\theta|<\tfrac{\pi}{4}$.  Denote $s:=\sin\theta$ and $c:=\cos\theta\;(>0)$.

\medskip
\emph{Step 1:  Ray integration.}
For $w\in S^{3}$ in the solid cone one has
\(
t_{\max}(w)=b/(u_{\theta}\!\cdot\!w).
\)
With the Euclidean surface element $d\sigma$ on $S^{3}$,
\[
\operatorname{vol}_{4}\!\bigl(\Omega(u_{\theta},b)\bigr)=
\frac{b^{4}}{4}\!\int_{D}(u_{\theta}\!\cdot\!w)^{-4}\,d\sigma(w), \tag{4}
\]
where $D:=S^{3}\cap\{\rho<w_{4}\}$.

\medskip
\emph{Step 2:  Evaluating the integral.}
A parametrisation of $D$ and two elementary Beta-function evaluations
give
\[
\int_{D}(u_{\theta}\!\cdot\!w)^{-4}\,d\sigma(w)
      =\frac{4\pi}{3}\,\cos^{-2}2\theta. \tag{5}
\]

\medskip
\emph{Step 3:  The volume formula.}
Inserting (5) into (4) yields
\[
\operatorname{vol}_{4}\!\bigl(\Omega(u_{\theta},b)\bigr)=
\frac{\pi}{3}\,b^{4}\cos^{-2}2\theta, \tag{6}
\]
so condition $(\ddagger\ddagger)$ is equivalent to $(\ddagger)$.

\bigskip
\textbf{(b) The first envelope.}

Put $m:=-\tan\theta$ ($|m|<1$); then from $(\ddagger)$
\[
b(m)=\kappa_{0}\,
      \frac{\sqrt{1-m^{2}}}{\sqrt{1+m^{2}}},
\qquad
\kappa_{0}:=\Bigl(\frac{3\pi}{4}\Bigr)^{\!1/4}a. \tag{7}
\]
Rotating $H_{\theta,b}$ about the $x_{4}$-axis yields the ruled surface
$(\heartsuit)$.

\medskip
Define
\[
F(m):=x_{4}-m\rho-\sqrt{1+m^{2}}\;b(m).
\]
A point $x$ belongs to the common envelope of the $\Sigma_{m}$ iff there
exists $m$ with $|m|<1$ such that
\[
F(m)=0,\qquad F'(m)=0. \tag{8}
\]

\emph{Derivative of $F$.}  
With $g(m):=\sqrt{1+m^{2}}$ one has from (7)
\[
b'(m)=-\frac{2m\,b(m)}{1-m^{4}},\qquad
F'(m)= -\rho-\frac{m}{g(m)}\,b(m)+\frac{2m\,g(m)\,b(m)}{1-m^{4}}. \tag{9}
\]

\smallskip
Equation $F'(m)=0$ gives
\[
\rho=\frac{m\,g(m)\,b(m)}{1-m^{2}}, \tag{10}
\]
and inserting (10) in $F(m)=0$ shows
\[
x_{4}=\frac{g(m)\,b(m)}{1-m^{2}}. \tag{11}
\]
Hence
\[
x_{4}^{2}-\rho^{2}=\frac{g^{2}(m)b^{2}(m)}{1-m^{2}}
                 =\kappa_{0}^{2}
                 =\Bigl(\tfrac{3\pi}{4}\Bigr)^{1/2}a^{2}, \tag{12}
\]
so the second envelope is $(\clubsuit)$.

\bigskip
\textbf{(c) General quadratic cones.}

Let $Q(x)=x\!\cdot\!A x$ with $A=A^{\top}$, ${\rm rank}\,A=n+1$ and
signature $(n,1)$.  The Lorentz dual form is
$Q^{\!*}(u)=u\!\cdot\!A^{-1}u$.  For the hyperplane
$\Pi_{u,b}:u\!\cdot\!x=b$ ($b>0$) define the truncated solid
\[
\Omega_{Q}(u,b):=\mathcal K_{Q}\cap\{u\!\cdot\!x\le b\}.
\]

\medskip
\emph{Volume of $\Omega_{Q}(u,b)$.}
Choose an affine map sending $\mathcal K_{Q}$ to the model cone

with (unit) normal $v$.  Because the map is linear, its Jacobian is a
constant.  A direct ray integration in the model cone gives
\[
\operatorname{vol}_{\,n+1}\!\bigl(\Omega_{Q}(u,b)\bigr)=
\kappa_{n}\,
\frac{b^{\,n+1}}{\lvert Q^{\!*}(u)\rvert^{\frac{n+1}{2}}},
\qquad
\kappa_{n}:=\frac{\operatorname{vol}_{\,n}(B^{\,n})}{\,n+1\,}, \tag{13}
\]
where $\operatorname{vol}_{\,n}(B^{\,n})=\dfrac{\pi^{\,n/2}}{\Gamma\!\bigl(\frac{n}{2}+1\bigr)}$ is
the volume of the unit $n$-ball.

\medskip
\emph{Equal-volume condition.}
If all truncations have the same volume $V$, then
\[
b^{\,n+1}=\lambda\,\lvert Q^{\!*}(u)\rvert^{\frac{n+1}{2}},\qquad
\lambda:=\frac{V}{\kappa_{n}}. \tag{14}
\]

\medskip
\emph{Tangent hyperplanes.}
Define
\[
p(u):=\lambda^{\frac{1}{n+1}}
       \frac{A^{-1}u}{\lvert Q^{\!*}(u)\rvert^{1/2}}. \tag{15}
\]
Then
\[
Q\bigl(p(u)\bigr)=-\lambda^{\frac{2}{n+1}},\qquad

\]
Hence $\Pi_{u,b}$ is tangent to the quadric
\[
Q(x)=-\lambda^{\frac{2}{n+1}}=:-A^{2}. \tag{17}
\]
Conversely, every hyperplane tangent to $Q(x)=-A^{2}$ satisfies
(14), hence belongs to the given family.  Therefore the equal-volume
hyperplanes are \emph{exactly} the tangent hyperplanes to (17).  An
affine unimodular change of coordinates transforms (17) into the
hyperboloid $(\clubsuit)$, completing the proof.

\hfill$\square$

\bigskip
